g.data-narration: Welcome to this lecture on Global States in Software Engineering. We'll explore why global state matters and how to manage it effectively.
Global state refers to data accessible from any part of a program, stored in global variables, static variables, or singleton objects that persist throughout application lifetime.
g.text: Data accessible and modifiable from any part of a program:
• Global variables • Static variables • Singleton objects • Configuration files
Unlike local variables, global state persists throughout application lifetime

g.data-narration: Global state is crucial in software testing because it affects test isolation, creates hidden dependencies, and can cause unpredictable behavior across modules.
g.text: In software testing and quality assurance, global state impacts:
• Test independence and isolation • Module interaction predictability

g.data-narration: There are four main types of global state: explicit globals like module-level variables, implicit state like environment variables, shared mutable state in concurrency, and singleton patterns.
Global state creates hidden dependencies between components. When Function A reads a global that Function B modifies, the relationship isn't visible in function signatures.
Global state makes testing extremely difficult because tests are no longer independent. A test that modifies global state affects all subsequent tests unexpectedly.
Global state introduces non-deterministic behavior. When global variables depend on operation order or external factors, program output becomes unpredictable.
g.text: In concurrent scenarios, race conditions cause different results:

g.data-narration: Debugging global state issues is complicated because incorrect values can originate from anywhere in the codebase, requiring extensive tracing.
g.text: Source of incorrect global value could be anywhere:

g.data-narration: Testing frameworks provide setup and teardown methods to save global state before tests and restore it afterward, ensuring test isolation.
Dependency injection passes dependencies as parameters rather than accessing global state, making dependencies explicit and testable.
g.text: def get_balance(): global current_user; return accounts[current_user]
Each test creates its own BankingSession with controlled state → No shared globals!

g.data-narration: Mocking and stubbing create fake implementations that return controlled values, allowing tests to manage what would otherwise come from global state.
Here's a banking example showing how global state causes test pollution, and how dependency injection solves it by creating isolated sessions.
g.text: def login(u): global current_user; current_user = u
Each test gets its own session: BankingSession("alice", {"alice": 1000})

g.data-narration: Pytest fixtures encapsulate state management beautifully. They provide known starting states and automatically clean up afterward.
Key strategies for managing global state include: minimize its use, use dependency injection, implement proper setup/teardown, and leverage mocking frameworks.
For exams, remember: global state creates hidden dependencies and testing challenges. Solutions include dependency injection, fixtures, and mocking. Modern practice prefers minimal global state.
g.text: 1. Define global state clearly | 2. Understand testing challenges | 3. Know solutions: DI, mocking, fixtures
4. Singleton = controlled global state | 5. Race conditions in concurrent global state
Modern principle: Minimize global mutable state for maintainability!
